trajectory.description: Observation: Starting fresh with no prior runs. Analysis: Need to quickly establish an end-to-end baseline using a strong pretrained model, standard label smoothing, RandAugment, and cosine LR to handle noisy CIFAR-100N labels. A short 5-epoch run on ConvNeXt Tiny will validate the pipeline and measure iteration time. Decision: Write model.py, train.py, predict.py; train 5 epochs; test predict.py locally with a fallback to val.pt; then submit eval.

Observation: Iteration 1 achieved 69.89% accuracy with ConvNeXt Tiny in just 5 epochs. Training loss was still strongly decreasing (1.78) and train_acc was 0.69 vs noisy val_acc 0.54, indicating substantial underfitting to the clean underlying distribution. Analysis: With ~40% label noise, longer training + strong regularization/augmentation like MixUp should improve generalization to clean test labels. The model is far from converged. Decision: Add MixUp augmentation, cosine LR with linear warmup, and scale to 30 epochs to capitalize on the strong pretrained backbone. Keep AMP enabled for speed.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 2 trained ConvNeXt Tiny for 30 epochs with MixUp/RandAugment. Training curves show train_acc rose to ~0.88 while noisy val_acc plateaued at ~0.52, indicating severe overfitting to the ~40% label noise. No eval was submitted for iter 2, so we don't know clean test accuracy yet. Analysis: With instance-dependent noise, explicit noise handling is needed. Cleanlab is pre-installed and can identify mislabeled samples using the already-trained model's predictions. Relabeling those samples and retraining from scratch should improve clean-test generalization. Decision: 1) Submit eval for the current best checkpoint to measure baseline. 2) Run a relabeling script with cleanlab to create train_cleaned.pt. 3) Launch a fresh 25-epoch training run on the cleaned labels with added stochastic depth (drop_path_rate=0.1) for extra regularization. 4) Update predict.py to use TTA (horizontal flip) by default for free inference-time gains.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 failed because relabel.py tried to write to the read-only /data filesystem. Additionally, cleanlab found 0 noisy labels because the 30-epoch model severely overfitted to the noisy labels (train_acc 0.88 vs noisy val_acc 0.52), making its predictions useless for noise detection. Analysis: We need a two-step approach: (1) get a baseline eval of the current best checkpoint to see if the overfitted model is actually worse than the 5-epoch model; (2) for label cleaning, we must use a model that has NOT memorized the noise—e.g., train for only 2-3 epochs with a frozen backbone, then use those predictions with cleanlab. Decision: Submit eval for the existing best_convnext_tiny.pt now. Simultaneously overwrite train.py with a robust version that supports --freeze_backbone_epochs and proper checkpoint metadata, and write a fixed relabel.py that saves to the workspace.

trajectory.command: python train.py --model convnext_tiny --epochs 5 --freeze_backbone_epochs 5 --batch_size 256 --lr 1e-2 --weight_decay 0.01 --label_smoothing 0.0 --mixup_alpha 0.0 --warmup_epochs 0 --checkpoint_dir checkpoints/noise_detector --save_prefix nd_ && python relabel.py --checkpoint checkpoints/noise_detector/nd_best_convnext_tiny.pt --output train_cleaned.pt
trajectory.description: Observation: The current best model (ConvNeXt Tiny, 30 epochs) achieves 72.67% accuracy but severely overfits to noisy labels (train_acc ~0.88), causing cleanlab to find 0 label issues because it confidently predicts the noisy labels. Analysis: To make label cleaning work, we need a noise-detector model that has NOT memorized the noise. Training only the classification head on top of a frozen pretrained backbone for a few epochs will learn the dominant signal without overfitting to all 40% noise. Decision: 1) Rewrite train.py with robust freeze_backbone support. 2) Train a 5-epoch frozen-head noise detector with no MixUp/label smoothing so cleanlab gets sharp, meaningful probabilities. 3) Run relabel.py using that checkpoint. 4) In the next iteration, train a full model on the cleaned labels.

trajectory.command: python train.py --train_pt train_cleaned.pt --val_pt /data/val.pt --model convnext_tiny --epochs 30 --batch_size 128 --lr 1e-3 --weight_decay 0.05 --label_smoothing 0.1 --mixup_alpha 0.2 --warmup_epochs 3 --drop_path_rate 0.1 --checkpoint_dir checkpoints/cleaned && python predict.py --data_path /data --checkpoint checkpoints/cleaned/best_convnext_tiny.pt --output /tmp/local_test.json

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 5 successfully trained a frozen-head noise detector and used cleanlab to relabel 10,895 training samples (24.21%) into train_cleaned.pt. The detector did not overfit to noise (train_acc 0.59, val_acc 0.55), so the relabeling should be meaningful. Analysis: The previous best clean-test accuracy is 72.67% from a model trained directly on raw noisy labels for 30 epochs. By training on the cleaned labels with additional regularization (drop_path_rate=0.1), we should significantly improve generalization to the clean test set. Decision: Rewrite train.py to be fully robust (support drop_path_rate, cleaned data paths, proper logging), then run a 30-epoch ConvNeXt Tiny fine-tuning on train_cleaned.pt. After training, run a local sanity check with predict.py on val.pt, and immediately submit the best checkpoint for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 6 achieved 69.19% accuracy with the cleaned-label model, which is worse than the 72.67% best from the raw-noise 30-epoch ConvNeXt Tiny (iter 4). The cleaned model likely suffered from cleanlab mis-corrections or overfitting to the newly introduced labels. We have only ~89 minutes left—too tight for a full 30-epoch retraining run. Analysis: Ensembling the two diverse checkpoints (raw best 72.67% and cleaned best 69.19%) should reduce variance and potentially boost accuracy because they were trained on different label distributions and make partially uncorrelated errors. No training time is required. Decision: Update predict.py to load both best checkpoints and average their logits (with horizontal-flip TTA), test locally, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Ensemble of raw-noise (72.67%) and cleaned-label (69.19%) ConvNeXt Tiny models with simple horizontal-flip TTA achieved 74.01% accuracy (new best). We have ~87 minutes and 23 iterations remaining—enough for one more training run but not many. Analysis: Before committing to a lengthy training run, we can squeeze free gains from stronger test-time augmentation. Replacing single-flip TTA with 10-crop TTA (FiveCrop + horizontal flips) should improve robustness on the 224×224 inputs without any training cost. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to use TenCrop TTA, run a quick local sanity check, and immediately submit evaluation.

trajectory.command: python train.py --model resnet50 --epochs 15 --batch_size 256 --lr 2e-3 --weight_decay 0.05 --label_smoothing 0.1 --mixup_alpha 0.2 --warmup_epochs 2 --num_workers 4 --checkpoint_dir checkpoints/resnet --data_dir /data --train_pt train.pt --val_pt val.pt
trajectory.description: Observation: The TenCrop TTA ensemble of two ConvNeXt models reached 74.53% accuracy (new best). We have ~84 minutes and 22 iterations remaining. Analysis: Adding a third architecturally diverse model should improve the ensemble via error decorrelation. ResNet50 (CNN with residual connections, IMAGENET1K_V2 weights) is fundamentally different from ConvNeXt (hierarchical backbone with depthwise convs). Training it for 15 epochs with batch_size=256 should take ~35-45 minutes, leaving time for evaluation. Decision: 1) Update predict.py to optionally include checkpoints/resnet/best_resnet50.pt in the ensemble (gracefully falls back if missing). 2) Launch ResNet50 training for 15 epochs on raw noisy labels with strong augmentation/regularization. 3) Evaluate the three-model ensemble in the next iteration.

trajectory.description: Observation: ResNet50 finished 15 epochs with best noisy val_acc 0.5292. predict.py already auto-loads any existing checkpoints from a 3-model ensemble list and applies TenCrop TTA. Analysis: With only ~38 minutes left, long training is impossible. The fastest path to a higher score is to test whether adding the architecturally-diverse ResNet50 improves the existing 74.53% two-model ConvNeXt ensemble. Decision: Submit evaluation immediately using the current predict.py pipeline; it will automatically ensemble all three available checkpoints (raw ConvNeXt, cleaned ConvNeXt, ResNet50) with TenCrop TTA.

Observation: Iteration 10 achieved 74.60% accuracy with a uniform 3-model ensemble (raw ConvNeXt, cleaned ConvNeXt, ResNet50) and TenCrop TTA. We have ~36 minutes and 20 iterations remaining, so time is the binding constraint. Analysis: Uniform weighting is likely suboptimal. The raw ConvNeXt alone scores 72.67%, while the cleaned ConvNeXt is weaker (~69%). A quick weight tuning on the cleaned training labels (train_cleaned.pt) should find better convex weights and yield a small but free accuracy gain without any new training. Decision: 1) Write tune_ensemble.py to grid-search ensemble weights on an 8192-sample subset of train_cleaned.pt. 2) Update predict.py to load and apply the tuned weights. 3) Submit evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 11 generated an overfitted ensemble_weights.json (weights [0.0, 0.8, 0.2]) by tuning on a training subset; it discards the strongest single model (raw ConvNeXt, 72.67% test) and will likely degrade test performance. We have ~35 minutes and 19 iterations left. Analysis: The best verified score is 74.60% from a uniform 3-model ensemble with TenCrop TTA. A cheap way to potentially improve further is to expand the ensemble with the 'final' checkpoints from each training run (raw, cleaned, ResNet). These checkpoints offer slightly different weight states and can increase prediction diversity without any training cost. Including 6 models total (best+final from each run) with uniform weights is safe because none of the checkpoints are near-random. Decision: 1) Delete the bad ensemble_weights.json. 2) Update predict.py to load all 6 available checkpoints and average their logits uniformly with TenCrop TTA. 3) Run a quick local sanity check on val.pt. 4) Submit evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The 8-model uniform ensemble achieved 76.73% accuracy (new best). We have ~18.7 minutes left and 17 iterations remaining—time is the binding constraint. Analysis: Uniform weighting is likely suboptimal. Because we have train_cleaned.pt (~45k samples with partially corrected labels), we can quickly learn ensemble weights by optimizing a softmax-weighted average of the 8 models' logits against those cleaner labels. With only 8 weights and 45k samples, overfitting is negligible, and the optimization takes seconds after extracting logits. This should improve over uniform weighting at virtually zero cost. Decision: 1) Write learn_weights.py to extract single-crop logits from all 8 checkpoints on train_cleaned.pt and learn weights via minimizing cross-entropy (Adam, 300 steps). 2) Update predict.py to load learned weights from ensemble_weights.json if present, else fall back to uniform. 3) Run learning + local predict sanity check in one chained command. 4) Submit evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The learned ensemble weights (favoring cleaned models heavily) produced the exact same 76.73% test accuracy as the uniform ensemble, indicating the weight tuning overfitted to the cleaned training labels and does not generalize. Analysis: With only ~2.2 minutes remaining, no training or lengthy local inference is possible. A quick, theoretically-grounded change is to switch from logit averaging to probability (softmax) averaging across models; this reduces sensitivity to models with different logit scales/calibrations and can improve ensemble robustness. I will also revert to uniform weights to avoid overfitted weights. Decision: 1) Overwrite ensemble_weights.json with uniform weights. 2) Rewrite predict.py to average softmax probabilities instead of logits. 3) Submit evaluation immediately with no local command to avoid timeout.
